title: Chapter 25 summary and quiz
summary_md:
## Chapter 25 Summary

This chapter covered virtual functions and runtime polymorphism in C++.

### Base pointers and the wrong version (25.1)

Calling a member function through a `Base*` or `Base&` resolves based on the static type, not the actual object type. The compiler picks the base version at compile time, ignoring any derived override.

### Virtual functions and polymorphism (25.2)

Marking a base function `virtual` defers resolution to runtime. The actual object type determines which version runs, enabling polymorphism through a single base reference.

### override, final, covariant returns (25.3)

`override` makes the compiler verify the function actually overrides a base virtual, catching signature mismatches. `final` prevents further overriding or inheritance. Covariant return types let an override return a more-derived pointer or reference.

### Virtual destructors (25.4)

Deleting a derived object through a base pointer without a virtual destructor skips the derived destructor, leaking resources. Any polymorphic base class needs a virtual destructor.

### Early vs late binding (25.5)

Early binding resolves calls at compile time (fast, inflexible). Late binding defers to runtime via virtual dispatch, adding one indirection per call.

### The virtual table (25.6)

Each polymorphic class has a vtable -- a static array of function pointers. Each object stores a hidden vptr pointing to its class's vtable. Virtual calls go through vptr to vtable to function pointer. Cost: one pointer per object, one indirection per call.

### Pure virtuals, abstract classes, interfaces (25.7)

A pure virtual function (`= 0`) forces derived classes to provide an implementation. A class with any pure virtual is abstract and cannot be instantiated. An interface class has only pure virtuals and a virtual destructor.

### Virtual base classes (25.8)

The diamond problem gives a class two copies of a shared ancestor. Declaring the shared base `virtual` in intermediate classes ensures one copy. The most-derived class constructs the virtual base.

### Object slicing (25.9)

Assigning a derived object to a base variable by value copies only the base portion, losing derived data and polymorphic behavior. Use pointers or references to avoid slicing.

### Dynamic casting (25.10)

`dynamic_cast<Derived*>(basePtr)` safely checks the runtime type, returning `nullptr` on failure (or throwing `std::bad_cast` for references). Requires at least one virtual function.

### Printing inherited classes (25.11)

Since `operator<<` cannot be virtual, the pattern is to have it call a virtual `print(std::ostream&)` member. Each derived class overrides `print`, and the single `operator<<` dispatches polymorphically.

exercises.title: Polymorphic Shape Container with Virtual Destructors
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Build an abstract `Shape` base class with pure virtual `area()` and a virtual `print()` method. Derive `Circle` and `Rectangle`, store them polymorphically in a `std::vector<std::unique_ptr<Shape>>`, and print each shape using `operator<<`.

## Input

- Line 1: integer N (1 <= N <= 20), the number of shapes.
- Next N lines: either `circle <radius>` or `rect <width> <height>`, where radius, width, and height are positive doubles.

## Output

For each shape in input order, print one line using `operator<<`:
- For a circle: `Circle: r=<radius> area=<area>`
- For a rectangle: `Rect: <width>x<height> area=<area>`

Then print a final line: `Total area: <sum>`

All floating-point values are printed with exactly 2 decimal places.

## Requirements

1. Abstract class `Shape` with: `virtual ~Shape() = default;`, pure virtual `double area() const = 0;`, virtual `void print(std::ostream& out) const = 0;`.
2. Free function `std::ostream& operator<<(std::ostream& out, const Shape& s)` that calls `s.print(out)` and returns `out`.
3. `Circle` derives from `Shape`, stores `double m_radius`. Overrides `area()` to return `pi * r * r` (use `M_PI` or `3.14159265358979323846`). Overrides `print` to output `Circle: r=<radius> area=<area>`.
4. `Rectangle` derives from `Shape`, stores `double m_width` and `double m_height`. Overrides `area()` to return `w * h`. Overrides `print` to output `Rect: <width>x<height> area=<area>`.
5. Use `override` on every derived virtual function.
6. In `main`, read shapes into `std::vector<std::unique_ptr<Shape>>`, print each with `std::cout << *shape << '\n'`, then print total area.
7. Use `<iostream>`, `<memory>`, `<vector>`, `<string>`, `<iomanip>`, `<cmath>`. Set `std::fixed` and `std::setprecision(2)` once at the start of `main`.

test_cases.label: Sample -- mixed shapes
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Circle: r=5.00 area=78.54
Rect: 4.00x6.00 area=24.00
Circle: r=1.00 area=3.14
Total area: 105.68

test_cases.label: Hidden -- multiple circles
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Circle: r=10.00 area=314.16
Circle: r=0.50 area=0.79
Total area: 314.95

exercises.title: Animal Hierarchy with Dynamic Casting and Slicing Protection
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Build a polymorphic `Animal` hierarchy with an abstract base, derived `Dog` and `Cat` classes, and a `Pet` interface. Use `dynamic_cast` to detect which animals are pets and demonstrate that virtual dispatch works correctly through base pointers.

## Input

- Line 1: integer N (1 <= N <= 20), the number of animals.
- Next N lines: either `dog <name>` or `cat <name>`, where name is a single word.

## Output

For each animal in input order, print one line via polymorphic `speak()`:
- For a dog: `<name> says Woof!`
- For a cat: `<name> says Meow!`

Then print a blank line, followed by the pet report. For each animal that can be `dynamic_cast` to `Pet*`, print:
- `<name> is a pet (trick: <trick>)`

where `<trick>` is `fetch` for dogs and `purr` for cats.

Finally print: `Pets found: <count>`

## Requirements

1. Abstract class `Animal`: `std::string m_name`, constructor taking `std::string`, `virtual ~Animal() = default`, `const std::string& getName() const`, pure virtual `std::string speak() const = 0`.
2. Interface class `Pet` (no data members): `virtual ~Pet() = default`, pure virtual `std::string trick() const = 0`.
3. `Dog` inherits from both `Animal` and `Pet`. `speak()` returns `"<name> says Woof!"`. `trick()` returns `"fetch"`.
4. `Cat` inherits from both `Animal` and `Pet`. `speak()` returns `"<name> says Meow!"`. `trick()` returns `"purr"`.
5. Use `override` on every overridden function.
6. Store animals in `std::vector<std::unique_ptr<Animal>>`. In the pet-detection loop, use `dynamic_cast<Pet*>(ptr.get())` to check if the animal implements `Pet`.
7. Use `<iostream>`, `<memory>`, `<vector>`, `<string>`.

test_cases.label: Sample -- mixed dogs and cats
test_cases.stdin:
4
dog Rex
cat Luna
dog Max
cat Milo
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Rex says Woof!
Luna says Meow!
Max says Woof!
Milo says Meow!

Rex is a pet (trick: fetch)
Luna is a pet (trick: purr)
Max is a pet (trick: fetch)
Milo is a pet (trick: purr)
Pets found: 4

test_cases.label: Hidden -- single dog
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Buddy says Woof!

Buddy is a pet (trick: fetch)
Pets found: 1

test_cases.label: Hidden -- three cats
test_cases.stdin:
3
cat Whiskers
cat Shadow
cat Nala
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Whiskers says Meow!
Shadow says Meow!
Nala says Meow!

Whiskers is a pet (trick: purr)
Shadow is a pet (trick: purr)
Nala is a pet (trick: purr)
Pets found: 3
